title: GitHub Review by greptile-apps[bot]
summary:
**Review Summary:**

1. **`RunJob` is unsafe across operator upgrades** — it treats a completed Job from a previous operator version as successful without checking if the spec (e.g., container image) has changed, so db-sync migrations silently get skipped. Fix by hashing the pod template spec into the Job name or detecting spec drift and re-creating the Job.

2. **Multiple `Ensure*` functions unconditionally update resources every reconcile** — `EnsureDatabase`, `ensureUser`, `ensureGrant`, `EnsureCertificate`, `EnsureDeployment`, `EnsurePushSecret`, and `EnsureCronJob` all call `Update` even when the spec hasn't changed, causing unnecessary API churn, spurious watch events, and potential 409 conflicts. Guard updates with a spec-equality check or use `controllerutil.CreateOrUpdate`.

3. **Deployment readiness test doesn't actually verify the `ObservedGeneration` guard** — the fake client never increments `Generation`, so the `ObservedGeneration < Generation` check is vacuously satisfied. The test needs explicit `Generation`/`ObservedGeneration` values and a separate case where `ObservedGeneration` lags behind to properly exercise the safety check.

code_comments.body:
**`RunJob` silently reuses a stale completed Job after spec change**

When an operator is upgraded to a new version with a new container image, `RunJob` looks up the Job by name, finds the previously-completed Job from the *old* version, and returns `(true, nil)` — treating the old run as evidence of success for the new code. The new db_sync migration never executes.

Concretely: if `keystone-db-sync` completed under operator `v1.0`, and the operator upgrades to `v2.0` (new image, new schema migration), `RunJob` returns `(true, nil)` on the very first call. The Keystone deployment is then updated to `v2.0` but runs against an un-migrated schema.

The `CreateImmutableConfigMap` function in this same PR solves the analogous problem for ConfigMaps by embedding a content hash in the name. The same pattern should apply to `RunJob`:
- Either name the Job with a hash of its pod template spec (so spec changes produce a new name → a new Job run)
- Or compare the existing Job's container image(s) against the desired spec and return an error / delete-and-recreate when they diverge

Without this, `database.RunDBSyncJob` is unsafe to use across operator version upgrades.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: internal/common/job/job.go
Line: 47-54

Comment:
**`RunJob` silently reuses a stale completed Job after spec change**

When an operator is upgraded to a new version with a new container image, `RunJob` looks up the Job by name, finds the previously-completed Job from the *old* version, and returns `(true, nil)` — treating the old run as evidence of success for the new code. The new db_sync migration never executes.

Concretely: if `keystone-db-sync` completed under operator `v1.0`, and the operator upgrades to `v2.0` (new image, new schema migration), `RunJob` returns `(true, nil)` on the very first call. The Keystone deployment is then updated to `v2.0` but runs against an un-migrated schema.

The `CreateImmutableConfigMap` function in this same PR solves the analogous problem for ConfigMaps by embedding a content hash in the name. The same pattern should apply to `RunJob`:
- Either name the Job with a hash of its pod template spec (so spec changes produce a new name → a new Job run)
- Or compare the existing Job's container image(s) against the desired spec and return an error / delete-and-recreate when they diverge

Without this, `database.RunDBSyncJob` is unsafe to use across operator version upgrades.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: RunJob (job/job.go:53-70) now guards against stale completed Jobs: after confirming IsJobComplete, it compares existing.Spec.Template.Spec against the desired job.Spec.Template.Spec using apiequality.Semantic.DeepEqual. When they differ (e.g. new container image from an operator upgrade), the old Job is deleted and a new one is created with the updated spec, returning (false, nil) so the reconciler requeues. Two new tests were added: TestRunJob_existingComplete_specChanged verifies delete-and-recreate behavior, and TestRunJob_existingComplete_specUnchanged verifies the happy path still returns (true, nil).

code_comments.body:
**Unconditional spec update causes unnecessary operator churn**

On every reconcile pass, `EnsureDatabase` calls `c.Update` even when the spec is unchanged. For a shared library consumed by multiple reconcilers that run frequently, this means:

- The MariaDB operator sees a steady stream of spec-update events and re-processes the Database CR on every reconcile cycle, even when nothing has changed.
- Every update increments the object's `resourceVersion`, which can trigger spurious watch events across the cluster.
- Under concurrent reconciliation, unnecessary updates increase the chance of `409 Conflict` errors.

The standard controller-runtime idiom for this pattern is `controllerutil.CreateOrUpdate`, which calls the mutate function and then performs a semantic equality check before issuing the actual HTTP PATCH. If you prefer to keep the manual Get/Create/Update pattern, a guard that skips the update when the specs are equal would avoid the churn:

```go
if !apiequality.Semantic.DeepEqual(existing.Spec, db.Spec) {
    existing.Spec = db.Spec
    if err := c.Update(ctx, existing); err != nil { ... }
    if err := c.Get(ctx, ..., existing); err != nil { ... }
}
return IsDatabaseReady(existing), nil
```

The same applies to `ensureUser` (lines 93–102), `ensureGrant` (lines 118–127), `EnsureCertificate` (`tls/tls.go` lines 43–50), `EnsureDeployment` (`deployment/deployment.go` lines 40–47), `EnsurePushSecret` (`secrets/secrets.go` lines 94–100), and `EnsureCronJob` (`job/job.go` lines 72–78).

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown

- Under concurrent reconciliation, unnecessary updates increase the chance of `409 Conflict` errors.

The standard controller-runtime idiom for this pattern is `controllerutil.CreateOrUpdate`, which calls the mutate function and then performs a semantic equality check before issuing the actual HTTP PATCH. If you prefer to keep the manual Get/Create/Update pattern, a guard that skips the update when the specs are equal would avoid the churn:

```go
if !apiequality.Semantic.DeepEqual(existing.Spec, db.Spec) {
    existing.Spec = db.Spec
    if err := c.Update(ctx, existing); err != nil { ... }
    if err := c.Get(ctx, ..., existing); err != nil { ... }

The same applies to `ensureUser` (lines 93–102), `ensureGrant` (lines 118–127), `EnsureCertificate` (`tls/tls.go` lines 43–50), `EnsureDeployment` (`deployment/deployment.go` lines 40–47), `EnsurePushSecret` (`secrets/secrets.go` lines 94–100), and `EnsureCronJob` (`job/job.go` lines 72–78).

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````

</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: All 7 locations listed by the reviewer now have an apiequality.Semantic.DeepEqual guard that skips the c.Update call when the spec is unchanged: EnsureDatabase (database.go:46), ensureUser (database.go:102), ensureGrant (database.go:134), EnsureCertificate (tls/tls.go:45), EnsureDeployment (deployment/deployment.go:43), EnsurePushSecret (secrets/secrets.go:101), and EnsureCronJob (job/job.go:96). Each guarded block also re-fetches the object after update to avoid evaluating stale status.

code_comments.body:
**Unit test doesn't exercise the `ObservedGeneration` guard on the update path**

`TestEnsureDeployment_readyWhenAvailable` exercises `EnsureDeployment` when the Deployment already exists. The function takes the update path, re-fetches after `c.Update`, and then calls `IsDeploymentReady`. With the fake client, `Generation` is never auto-incremented on a spec update (the fake client does not replicate this API-server behaviour), so the `ObservedGeneration < Generation` guard is always vacuously satisfied (`0 < 0 == false`).

This means the test does not verify the key safety property it was designed to check: that `IsDeploymentReady` returns `false` immediately after a spec change when the deployment controller has not yet caught up. Consider setting `Generation` and `ObservedGeneration` explicitly to simulate what the API server would do:

```go
existing := testDeployment()
existing.Generation = 2             // simulates a previous spec update
existing.Status.ObservedGeneration = 2  // controller has caught up
existing.Status.ReadyReplicas = 3
existing.Status.Conditions = []appsv1.DeploymentCondition{
    {Type: appsv1.DeploymentAvailable, Status: corev1.ConditionTrue},
}
```

A separate test case with `Generation = 2, ObservedGeneration = 1` would directly verify that the guard returns `false`.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: internal/common/deployment/deployment_test.go
Line: 120-145

Comment:
**Unit test doesn't exercise the `ObservedGeneration` guard on the update path**

`TestEnsureDeployment_readyWhenAvailable` exercises `EnsureDeployment` when the Deployment already exists. The function takes the update path, re-fetches after `c.Update`, and then calls `IsDeploymentReady`. With the fake client, `Generation` is never auto-incremented on a spec update (the fake client does not replicate this API-server behaviour), so the `ObservedGeneration < Generation` guard is always vacuously satisfied (`0 < 0 == false`).

This means the test does not verify the key safety property it was designed to check: that `IsDeploymentReady` returns `false` immediately after a spec change when the deployment controller has not yet caught up. Consider setting `Generation` and `ObservedGeneration` explicitly to simulate what the API server would do:

```go

```

A separate test case with `Generation = 2, ObservedGeneration = 1` would directly verify that the guard returns `false`.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: TestEnsureDeployment_readyWhenAvailable (deployment_test.go:128) now explicitly sets existing.Generation=2 and existing.Status.ObservedGeneration=2 so the ObservedGeneration guard is meaningfully exercised rather than vacuously satisfied at 0<0. A new test TestEnsureDeployment_notReadyWhenGenerationLags (deployment_test.go:152) sets Generation=2, ObservedGeneration=1 and verifies EnsureDeployment returns (false, nil), directly testing the safety property. Additional pure-function tests TestIsDeploymentReady_true (Generation=2, ObservedGeneration=2) and TestIsDeploymentReady_false_observedGenerationLag (Generation=3, ObservedGeneration=2) were also added.

resolution_summary: All three review comments were addressed. Comment [0] was fixed by adding a stale-Job guard in RunJob that compares the existing completed Job's pod template spec against the desired spec using apiequality.Semantic.DeepEqual, deleting and recreating the Job when they diverge. Comment [1] was fixed by wrapping every unconditional c.Update call in an apiequality.Semantic.DeepEqual guard across all 7 locations listed by the reviewer: EnsureDatabase, ensureUser, ensureGrant (database.go), EnsureCertificate (tls/tls.go), EnsureDeployment (deployment/deployment.go), EnsurePushSecret (secrets/secrets.go), and EnsureCronJob (job/job.go). Comment [2] was fixed by setting Generation/ObservedGeneration explicitly in the existing test and adding a new TestEnsureDeployment_notReadyWhenGenerationLags test case. Additional changes beyond the directly mentioned locations: the apiequality import was added to all affected files; two new RunJob tests (TestRunJob_existingComplete_specChanged, TestRunJob_existingComplete_specUnchanged) were added in job/job_test.go to cover the stale-Job guard; and four new IsDeploymentReady unit tests were added (TestIsDeploymentReady_true, TestIsDeploymentReady_false_observedGenerationLag, TestIsDeploymentReady_false_notEnoughReplicas, TestIsDeploymentReady_false_noCondition) that exercise the ObservedGeneration guard at the pure-function level.
